stdout:
OBSERVATIONS:
- (Cat 1, Img 1) No localized specular highlight anywhere in the frame — the surface only shows a broad, perfectly smooth luminance gradient with zero bright glint or environment reflection, even though the surface has visible relief.
- (Cat 1, Img 2) Sheets show distinct, localized specular glints (e.g. the bright near-vertical streak on the turquoise sheet center-left, small point highlights along the metal dowels) consistent with a real light source reflecting off a curved/wavy surface.
- (Cat 2, Img 1) The ripple texture is a near-perfectly regular, single-frequency wave pattern with uniform amplitude and spacing across the whole frame — reads as a tiled/procedural bump map rather than the irregular, multi-scale waviness real rolled glass shows.
- (Cat 3, Img 1) The cool-grey-to-olive color shift is a smooth, independent gradient overlay that doesn't correlate with the ripple bumps underneath — brighter/darker texture peaks don't shift color the way real thickness-driven glass tinting would.
- (Cat 5, Img 1) Illumination falls off as one clean top-to-bottom gradient with no secondary highlights, no room-light glints, no soft veil — reads as a single synthetic light rig rather than an ambient room environment.
- (Cat 5, Img 2) Lighting shows multiple contributions — overhead highlights plus softer ambient fill reflected differently per sheet color/angle, consistent with a real room.
- (Cat 6, Img 1) No visible sensor grain anywhere, even in the darker olive region; tonal transitions are banding-free and unnaturally smooth for a low-light capture.
- (Cat 6, Img 2) Visible fine grain in the wood shelf/dowels and mild softness increase toward the lower shelf, consistent with real camera noise and depth falloff.

VERDICT: Image 1 synthetic -- it combines a perfectly regular procedural ripple texture, a flat single-source lighting gradient decoupled from that texture, and a complete absence of specular highlights or sensor noise, none of which match real glass/camera behavior seen in image 2.
